articles.title: Ainapur records 44.7°C as heatwave intensifies in Kalyana Karnataka - The Hindu
articles.full_text:
Parts of Kalyana Karnataka continued to reel under intense heat with Ainapur in Chincholi taluk of Kalaburagi district recording a maximum temperature of 44.7°C, the highest in the State, according to the Karnataka State Natural Disaster Monitoring Centre (KSNDMC).
The State-level daily heat risk bulletin issued on Thursday noted that several locations in the region, including Santpur in Aurad taluk of Bidar district, have also recorded extreme temperatures touching 44°C during the 16-hour period ended at 4 p.m. on Thursday.
The district-level data showed several locations in Kalaburagi district recording high temperatures.
Kalagi recorded 44.6°C, followed by Kodli at 44.2°C. Meanwhile, Atanur (Afzalpur) recorded 43.7°C and several places, including Farhatabad (Kalaburagi), Karajgi (Afzalpur), Madana Hipparga (Aland) and Pattan (Kalaburagi), reported temperatures above 43°C indicating widespread and severe heat conditions across the district.
The KSNDMC has categorised taluks recording maximum temperatures above 42°C as under “alert”. In Kalyana Karnataka, this includes Aurad, Bhalki, Chitguppa, Humnabad and Kamalanagar in Bidar district, Afzalpur, Chincholi, Kalaburagi, Shahabad and Yadrami in Kalaburagi district, Arakera and Deodurg in Raichur and Gurmitkal and Wadagera in Yadgir district.
Several taluks across the region are under “monitoring” as temperatures have crossed 40°C, indicating widespread heat stress conditions.
The bulletin also warned that taluks with temperatures above 42°C are likely to face heatwave conditions and advised authorities to take precautionary measures in accordance with standard operating procedures.
Looking ahead, the forecast for May 1 to May 3 indicates continued heat stress across large parts of North Karnataka, including Kalyana Karnataka, with most districts falling under “moderate heat” risk.
Raichur taluk alone has been placed under “high heat” category during this period, while no region has been placed under the “extreme heat” category.
Advisory
With temperatures hovering around 45°C over the past three days, the district administrations across the region have issued special instructions for schools to ensure availability of drinking water and to avoid outdoor activities for students during peak heat hours.
They have also directed health authorities to maintain adequate stocks of ORS, IV fluids and ice packs at all primary health centres and hospitals and to be prepared to handle heat-related illnesses.
The administrations have also stressed the need for uninterrupted water and power supply, particularly in rural areas and public spaces, and have asked departments concerned to remain on alert.
In Kalaburagi, Deputy Commissioner B. Fouzia Taranum has issued a detailed public advisory to minimise the impact of heatwave conditions.
She urged people to avoid venturing outdoors between noon and 4 p.m. as far as possible. Those who must step out have been advised to use protective gear such as umbrellas, caps, footwear and sunglasses and to carry drinking water.
